nativeRange.habitat: Peat swamp forest pools and sluggish blackwater streams in lowland Sumatra; stagnant to very slow-moving tannin-rich water with extremely soft, acidic chemistry and little light penetration

decorPreferences: dense planting with shade-tolerant species (Microsorum, Cryptocoryne, Taxiphyllum)
driftwood roots and branches for cover and tannin release
dried leaf litter (Indian almond, oak) to acidify water and provide microhabitat
PVC pipe or clay cave/ceramic cave as spawning and refuge site
floating plants (duckweed, frogbit) to diffuse light and maintain humidity layer
dark substrate to reduce stress and enhance colour display

diet.notes: Feeds on small invertebrates in the wild. In captivity, offer small live or frozen foods as the dietary staple: Daphnia, baby brine shrimp (Artemia nauplii), bloodworm, micro-worm, white worm, and Grindal worm. Also accepts small insects such as fruit flies (Drosophila) and newly-hatched crickets. Some individuals accept high-quality small micro-pellets or flake foods, but a live/frozen diet is strongly preferred for long-term health and conditioning for breeding. Feed small portions once or twice daily; avoid overfeeding in low-filtration setups.

goodWith: conspecifics in bonded pairs or trios in a species-only setup
small peaceful Corydoras or Otocinclus in larger tanks with matching water parameters
small rasboras (Boraras species) if water chemistry is compatible
Kuhli loaches in shared blackwater biotopes
avoidWith: Betta splendens and other aggressive or fin-nipping bettas
larger or boisterous fish that will outcompete or harass them
shrimp (may be predated, especially fry-bearing females)
multiple males without ample territory in smaller tanks

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase reference image of Betta rubra male showing characteristic red-and-black barred pattern; confirm licensing before use.

summary: The Toba Betta is a strikingly coloured wild betta from peat-swamp blackwaters of northern Sumatra, Indonesia. Males display wine-red and black vertical barring with an iridescent blue pectoral patch. A paternal mouthbrooder in the coccina group, it is IUCN Critically Endangered and best kept as a dedicated species pair in soft, acidic water.
careNotes:
Betta rubra belongs to the coccina species group and is endemic to a narrow range in northern Sumatra (Aceh and North Sumatra provinces), with the type locality near Sibolga in the Lake Toba region — though it inhabits lowland peat-swamp drainages rather than the high-altitude lake itself. The species entered the hobby in 2007 and remains uncommon in the trade. Its critically endangered status in the wild makes responsible captive husbandry important.

Water chemistry is the most critical care parameter. B. rubra originates from extremely soft, acidic blackwater (pH 4.0–6.5, gH 0–5, kH near zero); hard or alkaline tap water must be treated with a reverse osmosis unit and remineralised to target values. Tannin-rich additives — dried Indian almond leaves, oak leaves, driftwood — help stabilise pH and recreate the natural environment. A peat-filtered or R/O-based water supply is strongly recommended.

A dedicated species tank of at least 40 litres (45 × 30 cm footprint) is suitable for a single pair. Larger volumes (60–80 L) allow a trio or small group with more stable water quality. Filtration must be extremely gentle — a slow-flow air-powered sponge filter is ideal — as this species inhabits near-stagnant water and strong currents cause stress. A tight-fitting lid is essential: B. rubra is an accomplished jumper and has the labyrinth organ typical of anabantoids, requiring a humid air layer above the water surface.

Decor should prioritise cover: floating plants, dense stem plants or epiphytes, leaf litter, and cave-like structures (clay pots, PVC) are all beneficial. A dark substrate reduces stress and intensifies coloration. Males display dramatic fin-spreading and colour intensification during courtship; females are considerably duller in coloration. Inter-male aggression can occur in confined space, so a single pair is the safest approach for smaller tanks.

B. rubra is sensitive to deteriorating water quality. Small, frequent water changes (10–20% weekly) with properly conditioned water are preferable to large infrequent changes. Avoid copper-based medications, which are toxic to anabantoids at standard doses.
breedingNotes:
Betta rubra is a paternal mouthbrooder. Unlike bubble-nesting bettas, the male incubates the egg mass orally for approximately 10–17 days until fully formed fry are released. Unusually within the genus, eggs are reportedly caught in the anal fin of the female before the male collects them for oral incubation.

Conditioning the pair on a varied live/frozen diet (Daphnia, Artemia, bloodworm, micro-worm) for 2–4 weeks before breeding attempts significantly improves spawning success. A cave or sheltered spawning site (upturned clay pot, ceramic tube) in a densely planted tank is recommended. Females of this species are notably active in initiating spawning.

Once the male is holding eggs, the pair should ideally be undisturbed and the male not fed (he will not eat while brooding). Fry are released at an advanced stage and are immediately free-swimming. First foods: micro-worm, Artemia nauplii, Grindal worm, vinegar eels, and powdered flake. Fry are relatively straightforward to raise provided soft, acidic water quality is maintained. Brood sizes tend to be modest (10–30 fry). Breeding difficulty is rated moderate due to the specific water chemistry requirements and the need for conditioning.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Betta rubra
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided water parameters (22–27°C, pH 5.0–6.6, hardness 18–90 ppm), tank dimensions (45×30 cm minimum for a pair), adult size (30–42 mm SL), diet, mouthbrooding behaviour (paternal; female catches eggs in anal fin), native range (Aceh and North Sumatra provinces), habitat (blackwater peat swamp), and compatibility guidance.

additional.name: FishBase — Betta rubra
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (5.0 cm TL), taxonomy (Osphronemidae, Anabantiformes), distribution (Indonesia, peat swamp), IUCN Endangered assessment date December 2018. FishBase lists reproduction as bubble nest but this conflicts with multiple specialist sources confirming paternal mouthbrooding — SeriouslyFish, TFH, and Aquadiction all agree on mouthbrooding; FishBase entry appears to be a data error.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — The Bold and Beautiful Betta rubra
additional.notes: Confirmed paternal mouthbrooding, brooding period approximately two weeks, diet (flake, live mosquito larvae, blackworms, whiteworms, Daphnia), habitat (stagnant peat swamps, low pH), labyrinth organ air-breathing requirement, tight lid recommendation, pair maintenance in ~19 L (5 gal) minimum. Male coloration: deep wine-red upper half, bright red and blue stripes lower half.

additional.name: Aquadiction — Toba Betta Species Profile
additional.notes: Confirmed temperature 22–27°C, pH 5.0–6.5, hardness 18–90 ppm, tank 45×30 cm minimum for two pairs, incubation 10–17 days, recommended foods (bloodworm, Daphnia, Artemia), decor preferences (driftwood, Microsorum, Taxiphyllum, Cryptocoryne). Noted as species-only tank recommended.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Toba betta
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Osphronemidae, Anabantiformes), endemic to Sumatra peat swamps, IUCN Endangered listing, adult length 5 cm TL, paternal mouthbrooder. Used for taxonomic cross-check only.

fish.breedingNotes: Paternal mouthbrooder; male incubates eggs orally for 10–17 days. Female initiates spawning and catches eggs in her anal fin before transferring to the male. Condition pair on live/frozen foods. Provide cave spawning site. Fry accept Artemia nauplii, micro-worm, and Grindal worm from birth. Brood sizes modest (10–30). Soft, acidic water chemistry required throughout.
